comments.body: I would check if any o is surrounded by any . so it's not in a garden. This may be a starting point. Then, treat edge cases
If a line (String) does not contain a fence - skip it. If it does : find the index of the first fence and the index of the second fence. Count moles in between those indices.
@cOder can you explain what you mean with coding sample
@c0der I spot a questionable assumption of convex garden in your approach.
I assume that the shown code does not contain any of your own. So ....

This, as most questions which give a strong impression of being a blind homework dump, lacks focus on the specific programming problem you encountered while trying yourself. To improve have a look at the compromise described here: meta.stackoverflow.com/questions/334822/… and at How to Ask.

Since the question has been closed I'll suggest a path here, building on c0der's hint and on Yunnosch's valuable comment: consider one line at a time, skip everything until you meet a vertical fence, check everything for a mole hill until you meet another vertical fence, and so on (gardens may have strange shapes)

To speed things up you may probably ignore lines 0 and 15 (and symbols 0 and 15 on the remaining lines) cause a fence cannot be on the border of the map; and you may probably also skip lines 1 and 14 since at most they can contain horizontal (top and bottom) fences
@Cid I am not sure that O|O|O|O|O represent a valid garden. If it does the algo needs to be adapted.
@c0der it's just a single line as you suggested to parse it line by line. I know a bit codingames and there are always such edge cases

answers.body:
Maybe my solution can help. I initially started with a floodfill approach however was stopped hard by the last test case. I then pivoted to a flag/switch approach to tell whether or not I was in the yard to determine whether or not a mole was valid. This approach worked for most cases until I the case of ++0+. Which would result in on off --> not count --> back on. After some tinkering and looking at the drawn picture I noticed a pattern on when to turn it on by comparing the current row to the previous row when bumping into an + or |.

Below is my code: (I've left my comments in the code to help readers understand my thought process)

class Solution {
    //the logic we need to implement here is that you need to have an opening and a closing wall to count 
    //the moles. We can indicate a start or end either with + or |. - are irrelevant bc we are counting left
    //to right. Not top to bottom. The bound of the stream will never contain a valid mole
    //If i am thinking about this right, we should never need to check the last line?
    public static void main(String args[]) {
        Scanner in = new Scanner(System.in);
        int moles = 0;
        //int validmoles = 0;
        boolean flag = false;
        char yard[][] = new char[16][16];
        for (int i = 0; i < 16; i++) {
            String line = in.nextLine();
            System.out.println(line); //O(n) run time, will realistically need to check the whole grid
            for(int j = 0; j< line.length(); j++){
                yard[i][j] = line.charAt(j);
            }
        }

        for(int i = 1; i < yard.length; i++){
            for(int j = 0; j < yard[0].length; j++){
                //Checking edge of fence and see if we have a valid mole
                if((yard[i][j] == '+' || yard[i][j] == '|') && (yard[i-1][j] == '+' || yard[i-1][j] == '|') ){
                    flag = !flag;   
                }
                else if(yard[i][j] == 'o' && flag){
                    moles++;
                }
            }
        }
       
        System.out.println(moles);
        
    }

title: A mountain of a mole hill?
body:
I am trying to solve https://www.codingame.com/ide/puzzle/a-mountain-of-a-mole-hill following problem but I dont understand how to approach to this problem. below requirement of the problem

Moles have infested a 16x16 area. The fences of your gardens are outlined on maps with |, + and - symbols. The mole hills are represented by a lower-case o. How many mole hills are in your gardens?

  • Two parallel fences of the garden cannot touch (it wouldn't make sense).
  • Garden space is filled with white space unless there is a mole hill. Other space is filled with dots unless there is a mole hill.
  • Everything that touches the outside of the map and is not enclosed in fences is other space. A fence is always between garden space and other space.
  • Mole hills cannot be on fences.

below initial code

        }

        // Write an answer using System.out.println()
        // To debug: System.err.println("Debug messages...");

        System.out.println("answer");
    }
}
